title: AI Agents as Assignable Team Members
topic: Integrating AI agents into MMFF Vector as assignable team members for stories and features, with safety rails, org-defined rules, and an MCP server foundation.
summary: How MMFF Vector can let product owners assign cards to AI agents through MCP, governed by configurable safety rails.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. The Vision — Agents as Team Members
  3. 3. How Assignment Works (Card Lifecycle with an Agent)
  4. 4. Safety Rails
  5. 5. Org-Defined Agent Rules
  6. 6. MCP Server Foundation
  7. 7. Competitive Landscape
  8. 8. The MMFF Differentiator
  9. 9. Implementation Roadmap
  10. 10. Risks & Open Questions
  11. 11. Sources
  12. 12. Gaps & Limitations

1. Overview

This paper explores what it would mean for MMFF Vector to treat AI agents — software programs like Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, or Cursor that can read instructions and write code on their own — as full members of a project team. Today, when a product owner moves a card on a kanban board, a human picks it up. The premise here is that the same gesture could send the card to an AI, which would then do the work and post a result back for human review.

The point is not to replace people. It is to give the product owner a single, familiar surface — the card — for both human and machine teammates, with the same accountability, the same conversation thread, and the same approval gate at the end. The technical bridge that makes this practical in 2026 is the Model Context Protocol, an open standard that lets any AI agent talk to any tool through a common interface. [1]

Our competitive scan found that most project management tools advertise "AI" but only do summaries, drafts, and rule-based automations. Two ecosystems — GitHub (with its coding agent) [4] and Microsoft Azure DevOps (which forwards work items to that same coding agent) [7] — actually let you assign a ticket to an AI that ships code. Everyone else assists humans rather than acting as an assignee. MMFF's opportunity is to build the agent-agnostic version of that loop, with safety rails configurable by a non-technical product owner.

2. The Vision — Agents as Team Members

Imagine opening MMFF Vector tomorrow morning. In the team panel beside Sarah and Marcus, you see "Claude — Backend Agent." Claude has a profile photo, a job title ("backend Go developer"), declared working hours (24/7, but capped at 8 active hours per day), a queue showing three cards in progress, and an activity feed: "Started PLA-0007/00301 at 09:14, paused at 09:32 to ask a clarifying question." It looks and feels like any other team member, because for the product owner's day-to-day work that is exactly what it is.

Concretely, an "agent as teammate" inside MMFF Vector means:

  • A profile. Name, avatar, declared skills (e.g. "Go backend, Postgres migrations, frontend React"), and the org-defined rules it operates under (its "constitution" — see Section 5).
  • A queue. The cards currently assigned to it, ordered by priority, visible to the whole team.
  • A status. Idle / Working / Waiting on PO / Blocked / Stopped.
  • An activity feed. A timestamped log of what the agent did, what tools it used, what files it touched, and what it asked for clarification on.
  • A budget. Token, time, and dollar caps per card and per day, set by an admin and visible to the PO.
  • A working address. The branch or worktree it owns. The PO does not need to understand git to use this — the UI shows "Claude is working on a sandbox copy" and offers a "show me the proposed changes" button.

For the product owner, the daily experience is unchanged in shape but expanded in capacity. You triage the backlog, drag a card to "Claude," and an hour later a notification says "Claude finished PLA-0007/00301 — 4 files changed, ready for your review." You click in, see a plain-English summary of what changed, approve or push back, and move on.

3. How Assignment Works (Card Lifecycle with an Agent)

The card moves through a small set of states, each of which corresponds to something visible to the product owner. The states are deliberately the same vocabulary used for human assignees — only the actor is different.

StateWhat just happenedWhat the PO sees
BacklogCard exists, no assignee.Normal card in the backlog column.
Assigned to agentPO drags card onto agent's avatar (or sets the assignee to the agent).Card moves to "To Do," gets a small bot icon, queue counter on agent profile increments.
Picked upAgent reads the card via MCP and decides to start.Card moves to "Doing." Activity feed entry: "Claude started, opening worktree branch agent/PLA-0007-00301."
WorkingAgent is reading code, writing changes, running tests inside its sandbox.Live status with progress notes ("3 of 5 files changed", "tests passing"). Token spend ticks up against the card's budget.
Waiting on POAgent hit a question it cannot resolve from the card or rules.Card moves to a yellow "Waiting" lane, comment posted to the card, optional notification.
ProposedAgent finished and opened a draft pull request, attached its link to the card.Card shows "Ready for review" badge, link to the PR, plain-English change summary.
AcceptedA human reviewed the PR and clicked Merge.Card moves to "Done." Audit trail closed, branch optionally deleted.
RejectedReviewer pushed back; either rework or close.Card returns to agent's queue with the reviewer's comment, or to "Backlog" if abandoned.

The crucial property: the agent never moves a card to "Done" by itself. "Proposed" and "Accepted" are two different states, separated by a human click. This mirrors how GitHub Copilot's coding agent behaves — it can draft a pull request but cannot mark it ready, approve it, or merge it. [5]

4. Safety Rails

This is the heart of the paper. An AI teammate without safety rails is not a teammate; it is a liability. Each rail below names a real risk, the mitigation, and what a non-technical product owner has to do to configure it. The pattern is borrowed from how GitHub describes its own agent's risks and mitigations [5], generalised so MMFF can apply the same discipline regardless of which agent the team chooses.

4.1 Non-destructive work guarantees

Risk: An agent can in principle push code straight to the live codebase, force-overwrite history, or delete branches — wiping out human work.

Rail: The agent can propose changes but only a human can publish them. Concretely, MMFF enforces three rules at the platform level: agents may only push to branches whose names begin with an agent prefix (e.g. agent/...); agents cannot push to main, force-push, delete branches, or skip pre-commit hooks; and every code change reaches production through a pull request that a human merges. GitHub's coding agent already works this way and we adopt the same posture. [5]

PO configuration: Nothing. This rail is on by default, organisation-wide, and cannot be turned off per-card.

4.2 Git status discipline

Risk: An agent starts work in a dirty tree, accidentally commits the human's uncommitted edits, or modifies files the human is mid-way through editing.

Rail: Every agent gets a fresh worktree (a clean copy of the codebase) when it picks up a card. It can read files freely if "read scope" is granted, but its writes happen in a branch it owns. The agent never touches the human's working copy. If the agent is paused, the worktree is preserved on a branch — nothing is lost.

PO configuration: One toggle per card: "Read access only" or "Read + propose changes." The default for spike/research cards is read-only.

4.3 Scope fences

Risk: The agent makes a small fix in the requested file but also edits 12 other files it noticed problems in, sprawling the change beyond what the PO asked for.

Rail: Each card declares a scope — a set of paths, services, or feature areas the agent is allowed to touch. Edits outside that scope are rejected by the platform, or the agent must post a comment asking the PO to broaden scope before continuing.

PO configuration: A "Scope" field on each card. The PO picks from a dropdown of declared services (e.g. "backend/users", "app/store/ui_apps/topology") rather than typing paths. Most cards inherit scope from their feature area label.

4.4 Approval gates

Risk: Code merges without a human ever reading it.

Rail: A human review and explicit merge click are mandatory. Optionally, an organisation can require a second-agent review (one agent reviews another's PR) before the human review — useful for catching obvious mistakes early without burning the human's attention.

PO configuration: Per-card or per-feature-area: "Reviewers needed: 1 human" (default), or "1 second-agent review + 1 human."

4.5 Budget caps

Risk: An agent gets stuck in a loop, retries the same failing test 200 times, and spends $400 of API credits on one card.

Rail: Every card has token, wall-clock, and dollar ceilings. When the agent hits any cap it stops, posts a comment to the card explaining why, and waits for the PO to either raise the cap or close the card.

PO configuration: Three sliders on the agent's profile (default per-card limits) and an override field on each individual card. The PO sees a running tally on the card and gets a notification at 80% of any cap.

4.6 Read-only mode

Risk: A research or spike card does not need code, but a default "agent assignee" might still produce a sprawling PR nobody asked for.

Rail: Cards labelled "research" or "spike" automatically run the agent in read-only mode: it can read code, comment, and produce a written report attached to the card, but it cannot create branches or PRs.

PO configuration: Set the card type. Read-only is the default for any card whose type is research, spike, or analysis.

4.7 Secrets posture

Risk: Production credentials end up in agent context windows or commits.

Rail: Agents never see production secrets. The agent's environment is provisioned with ephemeral, short-lived development credentials only. Every secret access is logged. Secret scanning runs on every commit before it can be merged. (GitHub's coding agent runs the same defence — secret scanning is one of its built-in checks. [5])

PO configuration: Nothing — admin-managed at the org level. The PO sees a "Secrets policy: production-isolated" badge on the agent profile.

4.8 Human accounts off-limits

Risk: The agent edits the password, role, or status of a real human user account while doing migration or test setup work.

Rail: Generalising the project's existing rule against modifying human passwords, the platform marks specific records or accounts as "human-owned." Agents can read them but writes are blocked at the platform layer. If the agent needs a test user, it creates a new one (e.g. agent-test-001@example.com) and never reuses a real human's row.

PO configuration: Admin marks rows or tables as protected. POs see a "Protected accounts: 3" indicator on relevant cards.

4.9 Audit trail

Risk: A bad change ships, and nobody can reconstruct what the agent saw, what prompt produced the change, or what tools it used along the way.

Rail: Every agent action is logged with a timestamp, the prompt, the tools called, the files read, and the files written. Commits are cryptographically signed and attribute the human requester as co-author. Logs are linked from the commit message and from the card. [5]

PO configuration: Nothing — always on. The PO can click "Show session log" on any card to read the full transcript of the agent's work.

4.10 Kill switch

Risk: An agent goes off the rails mid-card. The PO needs to stop it now, not in five minutes.

Rail: A single "Stop" button on the card. The agent's process is halted, its current work is checkpointed onto its branch (nothing lost), the card moves to a "Stopped" lane, and the PO can choose to resume, hand to a human, or close.

PO configuration: Visible to anyone with edit rights on the card. No special permission needed.

5. Org-Defined Agent Rules

Every organisation has its own taste, conventions, and red lines. A finance company might forbid agents from using third-party libraries without legal review; a games studio might mandate inline jokes in commit messages. None of this is encoded in the agent itself — it is encoded in a plain-English file that travels with the project.

The model here is the project's own CLAUDE.md file: a single readable document that sets out hard rules ("never modify human passwords"), preferences ("prefer Postgres over Mongo"), and pointers to deeper guides ("for styling, see docs/css-guide.md"). This file is the agent's constitution. It is checked into the repo, reviewed like any other code, and loaded into the agent's context every time it picks up a card.

For a non-technical product owner, the constitution does not require coding. It is just a structured document with sections like:

  • Hard rules. Things the agent must never do, in plain English, with rationale. Example: "Never delete a row from the customers table — soft-archive only."
  • Preferences. Defaults the team chose. Example: "Prefer functional React components over class components."
  • Pointers. "When working on the topology page, read docs/topology.md first."
  • Style guides. Naming conventions, comment style, commit message format.
  • Do/don't lists. "Do use the existing useDraft hook for forms. Don't reinvent it."

MMFF Vector should ship a constitution editor in the gadmin section: a structured form with the sections above, prose fields, and a live preview of how the file will look to the agent. Versioning is automatic — every save creates a snapshot. The PO can ask, "What rules did the agent see when it built this card?" and read the exact constitution that was active at that moment.

6. MCP Server Foundation

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI applications connect to external systems through a single, well-defined interface. Think of it as a USB-C port for AI: any agent (Claude Code, ChatGPT, Cursor, Copilot, etc.) can plug into any compliant server and immediately know what tools and data are available. [1]

Architecturally, MCP has three roles. The host is the AI application the user talks to (Claude Desktop, VS Code with Copilot). The client is the part of the host that maintains a connection to one server. The server is a program that exposes tools, resources, and prompts. Communication is JSON-RPC 2.0 over either a local stdio pipe or a remote HTTP channel with OAuth authentication. [2]

For MMFF Vector, this means we build one MCP server — call it vector-mcp — that any agent can connect to. The agent runs in its own process (Claude Code on the developer's machine, or Copilot in the cloud, or a self-hosted Cursor) and talks to Vector through the MCP server. Vector is the team membrane; the agent is the visiting craftsperson.

The Vector MCP server should expose, at minimum, the following tools. Each maps to an action the agent needs to participate in the lifecycle from Section 3.

ToolWhat it doesUsed in lifecycle stage
card.readFetch a card's title, description, scope, labels, attachments, comments.Picked up
card.list_assignedList all cards assigned to the agent.Idle / queue display
card.commentPost a comment to a card (progress note, question, summary).Working / Waiting on PO
card.moveMove a card between lanes the agent is allowed to set: Doing, Waiting, Proposed, Stopped.All transitions except Accepted
card.attach_prAttach a pull-request URL to a card.Proposed
card.request_reviewFlip the card to Proposed and notify reviewers.Proposed
agent.report_progressPush a structured progress entry to the activity feed and budget tracker.Working
agent.request_clarificationAsk the PO a question and pause the card. Uses MCP's elicitation primitive. [2]Waiting on PO
agent.request_budget_increasePolitely ask the PO for more tokens / time / dollars.Working (near cap)
agent.report_resultSubmit a final summary, files changed, tests run, and (for read-only cards) the written report.Proposed
org.read_constitutionPull the current org-defined rules file.Picked up
repo.list_filesList files within the card's declared scope.Working (read scope)
repo.read_fileRead a single file within scope.Working (read scope)

Notice what is not there: any tool to merge a PR, delete a card, modify a human user, or change the agent's own constitution. Those are deliberately off-limits — the agent cannot escalate its own privileges through the MCP surface. Linear's MCP server takes the same shape (it lets agents create/find/update issues and projects but does not hand over the merge button) [3], which validates the design.

7. Competitive Landscape

To frame the gap MMFF is targeting, here is what the major project management and dev-platform tools actually offer in 2026. The crucial distinction is between "AI features" (the tool helps a human) and "AI as assignable agent" (a card goes to the AI and code comes back).

ToolHas AI features?Can you assign a work item to an AI that ships code?Notes
GitHub (Copilot coding agent)YesYesSet the assignee of an issue to "Copilot." Agent creates a copilot/... branch, makes changes, opens a draft PR. Cannot push to main, cannot merge. Human reviews and merges. [4] [5]
Azure DevOps (Azure Boards)YesYes (via GitHub Copilot)From a Boards work item, click "Create a pull request with GitHub Copilot." Forwards the work item to the same coding agent. Code repo must live on GitHub. [7] [8]
Jira (Atlassian Rovo)YesNo (not for code)Rovo agents can create, comment on, and transition Jira issues; create/edit Confluence pages; bulk-update up to 20 work items at a time. The agent action catalogue lists no branch, PR, or code actions — code is handled by a separate "Rovo Dev" product. In automation rules, agents are further limited to text responses only. [9] [10]
LinearYesPartial — via external coding agents over MCPLinear ships an official MCP server that exposes issues, projects, and comments to any MCP-compliant agent. Linear itself does not run a code-shipping agent; you wire up Claude Code or similar through MCP. [3]
ClickUp (Brain)YesNoClickUp Brain auto-assigns tasks to humans, drafts subtasks, summarises meetings, prioritises, schedules. No code-shipping capability claimed on the marketing page. [11]
Asana (AI Studio / AI Teammates)YesNo"AI Teammates" drive marketing campaigns, manage IT tickets, support operations. The product page does not document code-shipping or git/PR integration; the framing is workflow automation, not software development. [12]
Notion (Custom Agents)YesNo (within Notion)Custom Agents run on schedules and triggers, perform multi-step workspace tasks, integrate with Slack and GitHub. The depth of GitHub integration described is read/notify rather than "open a PR for this Notion task." [13]

The pattern is unmistakable. Two ecosystems — GitHub and Azure DevOps via GitHub — let you assign a work item to an AI that ships code. Everyone else does AI assistance, not AI assignment. Even Atlassian's flagship agent product, Rovo, explicitly separates "agents that act in Jira/Confluence" from the code-writing product, and the in-Jira agents have no PR-creation tools.

8. The MMFF Differentiator

What MMFF Vector can build that no incumbent has shipped:

  • Agent-tool-agnostic. GitHub's solution only works if the agent is GitHub Copilot and the repo is on GitHub. Azure Boards inherits the same constraint. MMFF Vector should let any MCP-compliant agent — Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, a custom in-house agent — be wired up to any project, on any git host. The PM tool is the membrane; the agent is replaceable.
  • PO-configurable safety rails. The ten rails in Section 4 should be exposed as a friendly settings panel for non-technical product owners, not as YAML files an SRE has to author. GitHub's mitigations are good but described in developer-facing docs; MMFF should put them on the front cover of the agent profile.
  • Org-defined constitution at the team level. A first-class document, with a structured editor, that is the single source of truth for "how this team wants AI to behave." Travels with the project, versioned automatically, surfaced on every card.
  • A single human-accepts-the-PR loop, regardless of agent. Whether the work was done by Claude Code on a contractor's laptop or by Copilot in the cloud, the PO sees the same card states, the same activity feed, the same audit trail.
  • Read-only research lane. Most PM tools assume "task" means "code task." Vector explicitly handles spike and research cards as a first-class agent mode that produces a written report rather than a PR — closer to how product owners actually use their backlogs.

In one sentence: Vector is the team-membrane PM tool where any AI agent can be assigned a card, work within human-defined rules, and hand the result back through a single human-approves-the-PR gate.

9. Implementation Roadmap

A realistic four-phase rollout. Each phase is shippable on its own and earns its keep before the next phase starts.

PhaseGoalWhat shipsWhat it earns
1. Profile + read-only MCPAn agent appears as a teammate; it can read.Agent profile UI, agent-as-assignee on cards, MCP server with card.read, card.list_assigned, card.comment, repo.list_files, repo.read_file, org.read_constitution. Read-only research lane works end-to-end.POs can assign spike/research cards to an agent and get a written report. No code-write surface yet — minimum viable risk.
2. Code-write loop + budgets + auditAn agent can ship code through the human-approves-the-PR gate.card.attach_pr, card.request_review, card.move (to Proposed), agent worktree provisioning, branch-prefix enforcement, budget caps + 80% notifications, full session logs on every card.POs can assign coding cards. Most safety rails are now live. Agent-on-agent review still manual.
3. Constitution editor + scope fences + kill switchA non-technical PO can govern the agent without writing files.Structured constitution editor in gadmin, scope dropdown on cards, kill-switch button, scope-violation alerts.Org-wide AI governance is now a UI feature. Vector becomes adoptable by non-engineering teams.
4. Multi-agent + agent reviewVector supports a small team of agents; one can review another.Multiple agent profiles, second-agent review setting per feature area, automatic agent selection based on declared skills.Compounding velocity for predictable card types. Differentiates Vector from single-agent incumbents.

10. Risks & Open Questions

  • Agent loops. An agent that retries the same broken approach until budgets exhaust. Mitigation: budget caps (Section 4.5) and a "stop and ask" rule in the constitution. Open question: should we pattern-detect repeated tool calls and force a clarification request?
  • Bad PR cycles. Reviewer rejects, agent reworks, reviewer rejects again — a thrash that costs more than a human writing it. Open question: at what rejection count does the system auto-escalate the card to a human assignee?
  • Cost runaway. Budgets are per-card, but across hundreds of cards a day the bill can grow fast. Open question: org-level daily/weekly caps with automatic throttling?
  • IP / licensing of agent-produced code. Different agent vendors have different stances on training data and output IP. Open question: should the constitution include an "approved agents" list and reject assignment to others?
  • Hallucinated dependencies. Agents sometimes import packages that do not exist, opening a supply-chain attack vector. Mitigation: require dependency resolution to succeed in the agent's sandbox before a PR is opened, and run a known-package allowlist check.
  • Security review of agent-modified files. Some files (auth, payments, secrets handling) deserve human review every time, regardless of whether the change looks small. Open question: a "sensitive paths" list in the constitution that triggers mandatory human security review?
  • Prompt injection via card content. A malicious comment on a public card could try to redirect the agent. GitHub filters hidden characters before sending to the model [5] — Vector should do the same.
  • Attribution and accountability. When an agent ships a bug, who owns it? Mitigation: every commit shows the human requester as co-author, and the audit log makes the chain of approval explicit.
  • Agent reliability drift. Model updates can change behaviour overnight. Open question: should each org pin a model version per agent profile and only upgrade with explicit consent?
  • What if MCP itself changes? MCP is young (specification version 2025-06-18 was current as of writing). [2] Vector should treat its MCP layer as a versioned interface and be ready to support multiple versions for a transition period.

11. Sources

  4. GitHub Docs — About assigning tasks to Copilot coding agent

  7. GitHub Changelog — Assign Azure Boards work items to Copilot coding agent

12. Gaps & Limitations

  • Asana code-shipping. The Asana AI product page describes "AI teammates" in general workflow terms but does not document any code, branch, or PR action. We marked this "No" for code-shipping but cannot rule out a buried beta feature without deeper research into Asana's developer documentation.
  • Linear's own AI features. Linear's MCP support is well documented; Linear's own AI features (auto-summary, auto-draft) were not the focus of the changelog page we crawled and are not characterised here.
  • Rovo Dev capabilities. Atlassian separates productivity-app agents from a "Rovo Dev" product for code. We did not crawl Rovo Dev's documentation in this paper; the comparison row for Jira reflects only Rovo's in-Jira agent surface, which has no code actions.
  • ClickUp / Asana / Notion code claims. All three have marketing-heavy product pages that mention "agents" without crisply defining the boundary. Where we could not find a code-shipping action listed, we assumed there isn't one. A vendor-specific deep dive could shift these conclusions.
  • MCP version drift. The MCP spec version cited (2025-06-18) is the version surfaced on the docs page during research. The protocol is evolving; future MCP versions may add or change primitives we relied on (e.g. elicitation, sampling, experimental tasks).
  • No primary-source pricing. Cost projections for budget caps in Section 4.5 are illustrative — Vector's actual ceilings depend on the contracted rates with each agent vendor, which is out of scope for this paper.
  • Agent-on-agent review failure modes. Phase 4 of the roadmap suggests agents reviewing agents. We have not researched whether this materially improves bug-catch rate vs. costs an extra inference per card; that needs an empirical pilot.
